learning: When implementing managed one-shot continuations in Zig, run the first step on stack and only box state after the first real suspension to preserve a zero-allocation fast path.
evidence: zig build bench reported alloc_calls=0 for the no-capture loop and zig build run-effect-state plus zig build run-generator both passed in /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift
application: For future control-family features in shift, keep boxing behind the first suspension boundary and treat any no-capture allocation as a regression.

learning: When one-shot continuation wrappers can outlive their owner handle, prefer an explicit two-step Session lifecycle with close(mode) plus destroy() so teardown semantics stay safe without leaks or hidden global state.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the Session redesign passed zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-generator, and zig build bench while preserving alloc_calls=0 in the no-capture loop.
application: For future shift ownership changes, separate semantic shutdown from memory reclamation and keep destroy() gated on drained active continuations.

learning: When a two-step Session lifecycle is explicit, prefer one strict cleanup helper that fails loudly on unexpected close or destroy states because swallowed lifecycle errors can hide ownership bugs in continuation runtimes.
evidence: During the Session redesign in /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, strict cleanup plus the final green suite (zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-generator, zig build bench) replaced earlier silent catch-based teardown.
application: For future shift lifecycle changes, route cleanup through one strict helper and treat SessionBusy or SessionOpen during expected teardown as a bug unless the test is explicitly asserting it.

learning: When splitting lifecycle ownership in a Zig continuation runtime, prefer a strict close-then-destroy Session API because it keeps semantic shutdown separate from memory reclamation and preserves zero-allocation steady-state proofs.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the Session rewrite passed zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-generator, and zig build bench with alloc_calls=0 in the benchmark loop.
application: For future shift ownership changes, keep close semantics explicit, make destroy() validate lifecycle state, and re-check the no-capture benchmark after any owner-model change.

learning: When post-destroy continuation handles must fail cleanly in Zig, prefer a separate continuation control block plus explicit clone()/release() because plain struct copies are otherwise untrackable.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the continuation-control redesign passed zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-generator, and zig build bench while keeping alloc_calls=0 in the no-capture loop.
application: For future shift lifetime redesigns, move wrapper state out of the session owner and make extra handle references explicit instead of relying on invisible value copies.
